concepts.title: Physical and Chemical properties
concepts.conceptBody:
Group 15 Elements

Elements: N, P, As, Sb, Bi

Nitrogen and phosphorus are non-metals. The metallic character increases down the group due to lower I.E. and larger size. Hence, bismuth shows metallic character. The group state electronic configuration is $n{{s}^{2}}n{{p}^{3}}$.

Trends in Chemical Reactivity

Most common oxidation states shown by group 15 elements are $-3,+3$ and +5 . The stability of highest oxidation state $(+5)$ decreases down the group.

The covalent character goes on decreasing as we move down the group in the sequence, $\text{P}>\text{As}$ $>\text{Sb}>\text{Bi}$. This is due to increasing size of atom which refers to Fajan's rules.

$\text{Sb}$ and $\text{Bi}$ are the heavier elements of the group and form ${{\text{M}}^{3+}}$ cations due to decrease in ionization enthalpy.

In contrast to nitrogen, the phosphorus atom forms $\text{p}\pi =\text{p}\pi $ bonding and forms both cyclic and open chain compounds. Hence, it shows catenation.

As we move down the group there is a decrease is covalent character.

$\text{N}>\text{P}>\text{As}>\text{Sb}>\text{Bi}$

Nitrogen is chemically less reactive, due to high stability of its molecule. ${{\text{N}}_{2}}$ in which two nitrogen atoms are combined through a triple covalent bond $(\text{N}\equiv \text{N})$ of which one is sigma $(\sigma )$ and two are pie $(\pi )$ bonds, thus posses high bond strength $\left( 941.4\text{KJmo}{{\text{l}}^{-1}} \right.$ )

Nitrogen has one special feature that it can form $p\pi -p\pi $ multiple bonds with itself, carbon and oxygen due to its small size. In phosphorus rather $p\pi -p\pi ,d\pi -p\pi $ is found as in ${{\operatorname{POX}}_{3}}$.

General Trends in Physical Properties

Since, it is not easy from energy consideration to gain three electrons to achieve stable noble gas configuration, the formation of the tri-negative ion$\left( {{\text{M}}^{3-}} \right)$ is rare. But, nitrogen being the smallest and the most electronegative of all the members of the group, forms trivalent anion with reactive metals which have low ionisation energies. Thus, nitrogen forms ionic nitrides such as $\text{L}{{\text{i}}_{3}}~\text{N}$, $\text{B}{{\text{e}}_{3}}~{{\text{N}}_{2}},\text{M}{{\text{g}}_{3}}~{{\text{N}}_{2}},\text{C}{{\text{a}}_{3}}~{{\text{N}}_{2}}$. As the electronegativity of the other elements of the group, i.e., $\text{P},\text{As},\text{Sb}$ and $\text{Bi}$, is low, most of their compounds are covalent in nature.

Nitrogen is not able to extend its coordination number beyond four, the other elements have coordination numbers of five as well as six. The extension of coordination number is due to the availability of d-orbitals in $\text{P},\text{As},\text{Sb}$ and $\text{Bi}$, using which these elements can attain $s{{p}^{3}}~\text{d}$ and $\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{3}}~{{\text{d}}^{2}}$ hybridisation, e.g. in $\text{PC}{{\text{l}}_{5}}$ and $\text{PC}{{\text{l}}_{6}}^{-}$, respectively. Thus, nitrogen does not function as a Lewis acid whereas phosphorus, arsenic antimony and bismuth do so. The hybrides of this group and their alkyl derivatives act as Lewis bases, forming a number of addition complexes, e.g., $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$. $\text{B}{{\text{F}}_{3}},\text{CaC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}\cdot 4\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}}$, etc.

  • Stability of +3 state increases and that of +5 state decreases going down the group due to "inert pair effect", thus

$\text{B}{{\text{i}}^{3+}}>\text{S}{{\text{b}}^{3+}}>\text{A}{{\text{s}}^{3+}}$

$\text{B}{{\text{i}}^{5+}}<\text{S}{{\text{b}}^{5+}}<\text{A}{{\text{s}}^{5+}}$

$\text{B}{{\text{i}}^{5+}}$ salts are very rare and are good oxidising agents:

$\text{B}{{\text{i}}^{5+}}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{B}{{\text{i}}^{3+}}$

$\text{A}{{\text{s}}^{3+}}$ salts are good reducing agents:

$\text{A}{{\text{s}}^{3+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{A}{{\text{s}}^{5+}}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}$

  • They form trihalide and penthalide (except $\text{N}$, which does not form $\text{NC}{{\text{l}}_{5}}$ etc.) As we go down the group, covalent nature decrease and ionic nature increases; thus $\text{NC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$ and $\text{PC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$ are covalent while $\text{BiC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$ is ionic. This is also in accordance with Fajan's rule: Smaller the size of cation, larger the size of anion, greater the charge then greater the polarising power and hence greater the covlalent nature. Halides are hydrolysed in water forming oxyacids or oxychlorides:

$\text{PC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{P}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+3\text{HCl}$

$\text{SbC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{SbOCl}\downarrow +2\text{HCl}$

                   (orange ppt)

$\text{BiC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\underset{\text{ (white ppt) }}{\mathop{\text{BiOCl}+2\text{HCl}}}\,$

concepts.title: Oxides of Nitrogen
concepts.conceptBody:
Compounds of Nitrogen

Oxides of $\mathbf{N}$ 

(1) Dinitrogen oxide (or nitrous oxide) ${{\mathbf{N}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\mathbf{O}$

$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{N}}_{2}}\text{O}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Structure: It is isoelectronic to $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ and has linear structure. But unlike $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ it has dipole moment $(0.16\text{D})$. It shows resonance

(2) Nitric oxide (NO)

$\Rightarrow $ Copper reacts with dil $\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}(8\text{M})$ to form NO. It is reduction of $\text{NO}_{3}^{-}$

$8\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+3\text{Cu}\xrightarrow{{}}3\text{Cu}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+2\text{NO}$

$\Rightarrow $ Commercially, $\text{NO}$ is obtained by catalytic oxidation of $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ (ostwald process for $\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$)

$4\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+5{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow[\Delta ]{Pt\,\,catalyst}4\text{NO}+6{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Nitric oxide is a good ligand and form variety of complexes with transition metals which are known as nitrosyls, $\text{N}{{\text{a}}_{2}}\left[ \text{Fe}{{(\text{CN})}_{5}}\text{NO} \right]$

(3) Dinitrogen trioxide ${{\mathbf{N}}_{\mathbf{2}}}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{3}}}$

It can be obtained by mixing $\text{NO}$ and $\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ in equimolar quantities at low temperature.

$\text{NO}+\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow[\frac{Temp.}{(30{}^\circ F)}]{Low}\underset{\text{ (blue liq}\text{.) }}{\mathop{{{\text{N}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}}}\,$

In gaseous state it has the structure

The$\text{N}-\text{N}$ distance is very large, $1.864 Å$

(4) Nitrogen dioxide $\mathbf{N}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{2}}}\left( \mathbf{~}{{\mathbf{N}}_{\mathbf{2}}}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{4}}} \right)$

(i) $\text{Cu}+\text{ conc}\text{. HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

$\text{Cu}+4\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{Cu}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}+2\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

(ii) Di or trivalent metal nitrate decomposes on heating to$\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

$\text{Pb}{{\left( \text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{PbO}+2\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+1/2{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

Structure: $\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ is an odd electron (17 electrons) molecule and is paramagnetic. It has the structure

The unpaired electron ensures ready dimerisation to a diamagnetic colourless species ${{\text{N}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ (dinitrogen tetraoxide).

$2\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{N}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$

${{\text{N}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ is planar

(5) Nitrogen pentaoxide ${{\mathbf{N}}_{\mathbf{2}}}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{5}}}$

It is obtained by dehydration of $\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ by ${{\text{P}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{5}}$

$2\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+{{\text{P}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{5}}\xrightarrow{\Delta }2\text{HP}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+{{\text{N}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{5}}$

Structure: ${{\text{N}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{5}}$ is solid and in this state it is $\text{NO}_{2}^{+}\cdot \text{NO}_{3}^{-}$i.e. nitronium nitrate. In gaseous state it has the structure

concepts.title: Ammonia
concepts.conceptBody:
Ammonia -$\mathbf{N}{{\mathbf{H}}_{3}}$

It can be prepared in many ways:

(i) Any ammonium salt on heating with alkali forms ammonia.

$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Cl}+\text{NaOH}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+\text{NaCl}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O }$

$2\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OOCC}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+\text{Ca}{{(\text{OH})}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+\text{Ca}{{\left( \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COO} \right)}_{2}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

(ii) Ionic nitrides are hydrolysed by water to produce $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$.

$\text{M}{{\text{g}}_{3}}~{{\text{N}}_{2}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}2\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+3\text{MgO}$

(iii) $\text{NH}_{4}^{+}$salts which contain non-oxidizing anions, on heating given $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$.

$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Cl}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Cl}$

Non-oxidizing anions are$\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}},\text{SO}_{4}^{2-},\text{CO}_{3}^{2-},\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CO}{{\text{O}}^{-}},\text{PO}_{4}^{3-}$ etc.

However $\text{NH}_{4}^{+}$salts which contain oxidizing anions forms ${{\text{N}}_{2}}$ or ${{\text{N}}_{2}}\text{O}$ on heating.

$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}}(\text{l})\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{N}}_{2}}\text{O}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{N}}_{2}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

${{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}} \right)}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{7}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{N}}_{2}}+\text{C}{{\text{r}}_{2}}{{\text{O}}_{3}}+4{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

(iv) $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$ is manufactured by :

(a) Haber’s process ${{\text{N}}_{\text{2}}}\text{+3}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}\text{2N}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}(~\text{g})\text{+Q}$

(b) Cyanamide process

$\text{Ca}{{\text{C}}_{2}}+{{\text{N}}_{2}}\xrightarrow[\begin{matrix} furnace \\ \left( \sim 1100{}^\circ C \right) \\ \end{matrix}]{Electric}\underset{(\text{ Nitrolum })}{\mathop{\text{CaC}{{\text{N}}_{2}}+\text{C}}}\,\text{ }$

$\text{CaC}{{\text{N}}_{2}}+3{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\downarrow +2\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$

concepts.title: Nitric acid
concepts.conceptBody:
Nitric acid$\left( \mathbf{HN}{{\mathbf{O}}_{\mathbf{3}}} \right)$

$\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ can act as an oxidizing agent.

concepts.title: Aqua regia
concepts.conceptBody:
Aqua regia 
A mixture of conc. $\text{HCl}$ and $\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ in the ratio 3: 1 is called aquaregia. This is a powerful oxidizing agent and dissolves metals like $\text{Au}$ and $\text{Pt}$. The two acids on mixing react as
$3\text{HCl}+\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{NOCl}+2\text{Cl}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$
The presence of $\text{NOCl}$ and $\text{Cl}$ makes aquaregia a powerful oxidizing agent.
$\text{Au}+3\text{Cl}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{AuC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}$
$\text{AuC}{{\text{l}}_{3}}+\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{AuCl}_{4}^{-}$(or $\text{HAuC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}$, chloroauric acid)
$\text{Pt}+4\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{PtC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}$
$\text{PtC}{{\text{l}}_{4}}+2\text{C}{{\text{l}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{PtCl}_{6}^{2-}\text{ (or }{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{PtC}{{\text{l}}_{6}}\text{, Chloroplatinic acid) }$
Note: $\text{Au}$ is not oxidized by $\text{HN}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ alone but by aquaregia.
